intro: China's Ministry of Commerce unveiled a plan to use AI-powered products to stimulate consumer spending, marking a shift in economic strategy across the world's second-largest economy. OpenAI introduced Jalapeño, its first custom AI chip developed with Broadcom, as the company moves beyond relying solely on existing semiconductor providers. Nearly 400 local newspapers are suing OpenAI and Microsoft, claiming the companies scraped their content without permission to train AI models.

whitelist.title: China Wants AI To Make Consumers Spend Again
whitelist.desc: China's Ministry of Commerce and seven other agencies released a plan to motivate customers to buy new products with AI capabilities as a way of stimulating consumer spending.

whitelist.title: Meet Jalapeño: OpenAI’s First Custom AI Chip, Built With Broadcom
whitelist.desc: Jalapeño marks OpenAI’s move into custom chips, while Broadcom strengthens its role as AI’s infrastructure provider.

whitelist.title: Cloud-First Is No Longer Enough In The AI Era

whitelist.title: Nearly 400 local newspapers are suing OpenAI and Microsoft.
whitelist.desc: The coalition of publishers alleges OpenAI and Microsoft “scraped, copied, and ingested” their work without permission or compensation to train their AI models. This latest legal battle adds to the string of copyright lawsuits OpenAI is facing from publishers like The New York Times, Ziff Davis, Merriam-Webster, and Encyclopedia Britannica. [Link: OpenAI, Microsoft Sued by Publishers for Scraping News Articles | https://news.bloomberglaw.com/us-law-week/publishers-sue-microsoft-openai-over-unaut...
